topic: A student learns their scholarship application was denied
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email sat open on the screen, a stark white rectangle mocking me. "We regret to inform you that your application for the [Scholarship Name] has not been successful." My fingers hovered over the keyboard, trembling slightly. The air in my small dorm room suddenly felt thick, suffocating. I reread the rejection letter, each word a tiny, sharp pinprick against my chest. I quickly minimized the window, shoving the laptop under my bed as if the screen itself held some contagious stain. When my roommate knocked, I forced a bright, almost brittle smile, turning my attention immediately to rearranging my bookshelf, meticulously aligning every spine. The silence of the room amplified the frantic drumming in my own ears.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the darkened windowpane. The face staring back looked pale, drawn, as if someone had meticulously scraped away the color. Every time I moved, there was a stiffness in my posture, a deliberate attempt to shrink my frame. The rejection notice felt like a physical weight pressing down on my shoulders, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I spent the afternoon pacing the length of my room, the worn carpet muffling my footsteps, a restless energy churning beneath my skin. When I finally sat down to study, the textbook swam before my eyes, the words refusing to cohere into anything meaningful. I just stared at the blank page, unable to focus on anything but the stinging disappointment.

[story 3]

The congratulatory notes from other applicants, which I had almost ignored, suddenly felt like accusations. I found myself compulsively checking my old submission documents, re-reading every essay, searching for some invisible flaw, some oversight that could have derailed the entire process. My jaw tightened until it ached, and I bit down hard on the inside of my cheek, tasting copper. I tried to engage with my friends, offering forced laughter, but the sound felt hollow, disconnected from the heavy knot in my stomach. Later that evening, I walked aimlessly through the campus quad, the cool night air doing nothing to soothe the heat rising in my cheeks. Each passing face seemed to highlight the gulf between where I stood and where I desperately wanted to be.
